narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 47% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic indicators like grades, test scores, and background factors, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The main driver pushing the model toward predicting failure was your LSAT score of 24, which stands well below the average of about 37 for students who went on to pass the bar, making it the single strongest factor in this outcome. On a more positive note, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 7 outperformed the typical rank of around 6 for successful bar takers, which helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 7 exceeded the average of about 6 for those who passed, providing another counterbalancing influence that softened the overall risk assessment. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average among bar passers, so it played a neutral role here. Coming from a middle-income family aligns with over a third of those who succeeded, and planning full-time study matches nearly all successful students, but these didn't outweigh the key concerns. Compared to admitted applicants, who typically show stronger LSAT performance alongside solid rankings like yours, your profile fell short primarily on that test score metric. While your academic deciles suggest you're competitive in a school setting, the model prioritizes bar exam predictors where LSAT carries heavy weight. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, as that could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. You might also gather letters highlighting your Year 1 and Year 3 strengths or explore programs with flexible admission criteria. Keep pursuing your goals; many students improve their profiles and succeed on later attempts.
